turns.content: right so i need help planning my gym schedule around work because its getting bare chaotic rn
Happy to help! Can you tell me a bit about your current situation — what does your work schedule look like, and how often are you trying to work out?

ok so i work in accounting yeah, audit specifically, and the hours are not normal. like on a good week im done by 6 or 7 but busy season is coming and last year i was doing 7am to like 10pm some nights. i try to get to the gym 3 times a week, thats my goal, but when work goes mad it just doesnt happen

That's a huge swing in schedule — going from finishing at 6-7pm to potentially 10pm makes it really hard to maintain consistency. Which gym do you go to, and what are their hours? That might help us figure out when you can realistically fit sessions in.
PureGym, the one near Piccadilly. its 24 hours which is the only reason i even considered it tbh. its like a 10 min walk from my office so i can go straight after work on normal days

The 24-hour access is a huge advantage here. Since it's only 10 minutes from your office, you have a few options even during busy season:

1. **Early morning sessions** — Could you go before work? Even a 30-40 minute session at 6am would count
2. **Lunch break workouts** — If you get even 45 minutes for lunch, a quick session is doable given how close it is
3. **Late night sessions** — If you finish at 10pm, a quick 30 minutes before heading home

Which of those feels most realistic for you?

loool the idea of me voluntarily waking up at 5:30am to go to the gym is actually hilarious. im NOT a morning person wallah. lunch breaks during busy season dont really exist either, you eat at your desk while working. the after work thing is what i do normally — finish at 6ish, walk to PureGym, do like an hour, go home. thats the routine when things are calm

Ha, fair enough — if mornings aren't happening, forcing it will just make you miserable and you'll stop going entirely. So your current routine of after-work sessions 3 times a week sounds solid for normal periods.

For busy season, what about a scaled-down approach? Instead of trying to maintain the full 3x/week hour-long sessions, you could:

- **Drop to 2x/week** with shorter 30-40 minute sessions
- **Move to weekends** — Saturday and Sunday sessions when you're not working
- **Late night quick hits** — even 20 minutes of compound lifts after a long day is better than nothing

What does your weekend look like during busy season?

weekends during busy season... i usually have saturday off but sometimes i have to go in. sunday is always free though alhamdulillah. yeah i think maybe doing 2 proper sessions on the weekend and then trying to squeeze one in during the week might work? like monday or tuesday evening before it gets too mental

That's a smart approach — front-loading the week with one midweek session (Monday or Tuesday) before the workload peaks, then catching up on the weekend. A few tips to make this stick:

- **Monday is probably better than Tuesday** — you're more likely to still have energy and motivation at the start of the week
- **Pack your gym bag the night before** and keep it at work — removes the excuse of "I'd have to go home first"
- **Weekend sessions can be longer** (60-75 min) to make up for the shorter midweek one

What kind of workouts are you doing? Weights, cardio, classes?

mix tbh. i do weights — mostly lower body and some upper body. and then like 15-20 mins cardio at the end, usually the stairmaster because the treadmill is dead boring. ive been trying to get more into strength training but i feel like i dont really know what im doing half the time loool. like i watch videos on youtube and try to copy the exercises but idk if my form is right

That's really common — YouTube is great for ideas but terrible for form feedback because you can't see yourself the way the camera sees them. A few options:

1. **Film yourself** — Prop your phone up and record your sets. Compare your form side-by-side with a tutorial. You'll spot issues immediately
2. **PureGym PT intro session** — Most PureGyms offer a free introductory session or assessment when you join. If you haven't used yours, book it
3. **Focus on a few key lifts** — Rather than trying to learn 15 exercises, nail the fundamentals: squats, hip thrusts, Romanian deadlifts, overhead press. Master those and everything else becomes easier

For lower body specifically, what exercises are you currently doing?

squats on the smith machine, leg press, hip thrusts with a barbell (these are so awkward to set up on my own btw), and sometimes lunges when i can be bothered. for upper body i just do like lat pulldowns and some dumbbell stuff but nothing structured

Solid foundation! A few thoughts:

**Lower body:** Your exercise selection is good. One thing — if you can, try transitioning from Smith machine squats to free barbell squats over time. The Smith machine locks you into a fixed path which doesn't develop the stabilizer muscles. Start light on the free bar, even just with the empty barbell, and build up.

For hip thrusts — try the setup where you roll the barbell over your legs with a thick pad. Or if your PureGym has a dedicated hip thrust machine, use that instead. Way less awkward than rigging it yourself.

**Upper body:** Here's a simple structure to add:
- **Push day:** Dumbbell bench press, overhead press, tricep pushdowns
- **Pull day:** Lat pulldowns (already doing), seated rows, bicep curls

Want me to put together a simple 3-day split you can follow? One upper, one lower, one full body?
yes please that would be so helpful. nothing too complicated tho because honestly if its like a 15 exercise plan im just gonna look at it and panic. keep it simple init

Keeping it simple is the key to actually sticking with it. Here's a clean 3-day split:

**Day 1 — Lower Body (Weekend)**
- Squats (barbell or Smith): 4x8-10
- Hip thrusts: 3x10-12
- Romanian deadlifts: 3x10
- Walking lunges: 3x12 each leg
- Stairmaster: 15 min

**Day 2 — Upper Body (Weekend)**
- Lat pulldowns: 3x10-12
- Seated cable rows: 3x10-12
- Dumbbell shoulder press: 3x10
- Dumbbell bench press: 3x10
- Bicep curls + Tricep pushdowns superset: 3x12 each
- Stairmaster: 15 min

**Day 3 — Full Body (Midweek — shorter session)**
- Squats: 3x8
- Hip thrusts: 3x10
- Lat pulldowns: 3x10
- Dumbbell shoulder press: 3x10
- Stairmaster: 10 min

The midweek session is deliberately shorter — 5 exercises and quick cardio, you're in and out in 40 minutes. The weekend ones are around 50-60 minutes each. All manageable?

this is actually perfect. not overwhelming at all. im saving this. i think the midweek one is especially good because if im knackered from work at least its short and i can just get it done and go home. wallah thank you this is way better than my current approach of just wandering around the gym doing random exercises

Ha, the "gym wandering" phase is real — everyone does it. Having a plan means you walk in, know exactly what you're doing, and walk out. No decision fatigue.

A couple of final tips:
- **Track your weights** — even just in your phone notes. Write down what you lifted each session so you can gradually increase
- **Don't skip the warm-up** — 5 minutes on the bike or a few bodyweight squats before you start. Especially important after sitting at a desk all day
- **If busy season kills a session, don't try to "make it up"** — just pick up where you left off. Consistency over time beats perfection in any single week

Good luck with it! Sounds like you've got a solid plan for keeping the routine alive even when work gets intense.
